rescued_person_information.place_where_action_occurred: South Platte River, section near Carson Nature Preserve, about 2 miles west of Littleton, CO

summary_and_certification.summary_of_the_action:
On Saturday, 2024-06-15, during a scheduled Troop 742 day hike and river ecology outing along the South Platte River near the Carson Nature Preserve outside Littleton, Colorado, Star Scout Ethan Michael Carter, age 14 at the time, performed a prompt and well-executed water rescue of fellow Scout Jacob Andrew Miller, age 13, who had been swept into fast-moving, near-chest-deep water.

The troop, consisting of 11 youth and 4 registered adult leaders, had stopped at a shallow bend in the river to conduct a planned activity identifying macroinvertebrates and practicing Leave No Trace principles near the shoreline. The river level was moderately elevated due to recent snowmelt and afternoon thunderstorms earlier in the week. Prior to approaching the water, the Scoutmaster and assistant Scoutmasters reviewed the safety boundaries with the youth, clearly designating a shallow, calmer area as the only approved wading zone and instructing all Scouts to keep their boots on, stay below knee depth, and to use the buddy system.

Jacob, while attempting to reach a partially submerged rock to inspect insect larvae, stepped beyond the designated boundary onto a slick, algae-covered rock shelf. Witnesses, including two other Scouts and Assistant Scoutmaster Mark Reynolds, report that Jacob lost his footing suddenly when the rock shifted under him. He fell sideways into a deeper channel, where the river depth dropped rapidly from mid-shin to approximately chest level on his frame. The current in this channel was noticeably swifter and immediately pushed him downstream toward a cluster of partially submerged boulders and low-hanging branches.

Jacob resurfaced in visible distress, coughing and calling for help. Multiple witnesses, including Scouts and adults, confirm that Jacob appeared to be struggling to keep his head above water and was being carried sideways by the current toward a strainers-like area of branches and rocks that could have trapped him underwater. Ethan, who was standing on the bank removing his daypack after being dismissed from the water activity, immediately recognized the danger and responded without hesitation but with clear evidence of training and forethought.

In the months prior, Ethan had completed the BSA Swimming merit badge, the Lifesaving merit badge, and a community-based Red Cross Basic Water Rescue course. Those who observed his actions consistently noted that he did not simply jump in impulsively; instead, he quickly applied the sequence of reach-throw-row-go that is emphasized in BSA safety training.

Upon hearing Jacob’s call for help and seeing him pulled into deeper water, Ethan first shouted to the nearest adult leader, yelling, "Mr. Reynolds, Jacob’s in the current!" to ensure that adult attention was focused on the emergency. Almost simultaneously, he instructed two nearby Scouts, "Go get Mr. Carter and the first-aid kit now!" thereby delegating tasks and initiating an organized response. Witnesses confirm that this happened within seconds of Jacob entering the deep water.

Ethan then ran to a section of the bank slightly downstream of Jacob’s position where the current bent closer to shore. There, he quickly located a long, partially dried willow branch approximately 8–9 feet in length. He lay down flat on his stomach on the riverbank to lower his center of gravity, anchoring his feet against a large rock and bracing his upper body. From this position, he extended the branch out over the water and loudly instructed Jacob to "grab the end of the stick and hold on tight." Jacob, still coughing and intermittently going under, initially missed the branch on the first attempt due to the current.

Recognizing that Jacob might not be able to coordinate well enough to grasp the branch unaided, Ethan adjusted his position. He scooted closer to the edge while still remaining prone and braced, and he extended the branch further into the current, timing his reach so that the branch intercepted Jacob as he was swept past. On the second attempt, the branch made contact with Jacob’s chest and arm. Ethan shouted, "Wrap your arm around it!" Jacob managed to hook his right arm over the branch and partially around it.

At that point, Ethan began carefully hauling Jacob toward shore, hand over hand along the length of the branch, continuing to speak calmly and clearly, telling Jacob to kick his feet and keep his face up. Assistant Scoutmaster Reynolds, who had been moving rapidly toward them after Ethan’s initial shout, arrived as Ethan had pulled Jacob to within about 3–4 feet of the bank. Mr. Reynolds then waded into the shallower edge, grasped the back of Jacob’s daypack and collar, and, with Ethan’s help, lifted Jacob fully out of the current and onto the grassy bank.

Once Jacob was out of the water, Ethan immediately shifted his attention to assessing Jacob’s condition, consistent with his first-aid and lifesaving training. Witnesses report that Ethan checked to make sure Jacob was breathing, asked him simple questions to determine his level of responsiveness, and directed another Scout to bring over Jacob’s dry sweatshirt and a towel from a nearby gear pile. Ethan encouraged Jacob to lie on his side initially while he coughed up water and then assisted in helping Jacob into a sitting position once his breathing became more regular. He also advised that Jacob’s wet clothing be replaced with dry layers to prevent hypothermia, as the river water was cold from snowmelt.

During this period, Ethan remained calm, speaking reassuringly to Jacob, telling him, "You’re okay, just keep breathing slow; we’ve got you." He also reminded the other Scouts to give Jacob space and stay back so the adults could work, reducing crowding and panic. While Assistant Scoutmaster Reynolds contacted 911 using his cell phone to report the incident and request a precautionary medical evaluation, Ethan relayed relevant information about what he had seen: approximately how long Jacob had been in the deeper water (witnesses estimate 20–30 seconds), whether Jacob had gone fully underwater (he had briefly submerged twice), and whether he had struck his head or any rocks (no such impact was observed by Ethan or others).

Emergency medical services arrived at the nearby trailhead approximately 18–20 minutes later. During the wait, Ethan continued to monitor Jacob, watching for signs of worsening distress, such as increasing difficulty breathing, confusion, or excessive lethargy. He periodically asked Jacob simple orientation questions (his name, the date, and where they were) and reported Jacob’s responses to Assistant Scoutmaster Reynolds, who relayed them to EMS when they arrived. Jacob remained conscious and talking, though shaken and tired.

The paramedics who evaluated Jacob at the scene noted that he had likely aspirated a small amount of water and was at risk for secondary complications; they recommended transport to the hospital for further observation. Several witnesses, including two other Scouts (Brandon Liu and Carlos Ramirez), the Scoutmaster (Daniel Harris), and Assistant Scoutmaster Reynolds, have independently stated that had Ethan not reacted as quickly and as methodically as he did—moving downstream to intercept Jacob with a reaching assist instead of entering the water directly—Jacob could have been pushed further into the faster, rockier section of the river. That area contained multiple submerged obstacles and strainers that posed a serious risk of entrapment, head injury, or drowning.

Importantly, Ethan did not endanger himself or others by impulsively jumping into the deep, fast-moving water. Instead, he followed BSA Safe Swim Defense principles by attempting a reach rescue from a stable position using an improvised extension device (the willow branch). He maintained clear communication with both the victim and the adult leaders, organized his peers to obtain help and supplies, and remained actively engaged in post-rescue monitoring until EMS personnel formally took over care.

The council committee, after reviewing written statements from all four adult leaders present, three youth witnesses, and the EMS incident summary, finds that Ethan’s actions demonstrated presence of mind, appropriate application of his Scouting and lifesaving training, and a willingness to act promptly in the face of another person’s imminent danger. The committee concludes that Ethan’s conduct directly prevented a potentially serious or fatal drowning incident and that his actions were above and beyond what might reasonably be expected of a Scout of his age and experience, while still remaining consistent with BSA safety guidelines. For these reasons, the committee believes that Ethan Michael Carter’s actions merit formal recognition under the Boy Scouts of America’s lifesaving and meritorious action awards program.
